title: Clockify vs eHour for Non-technical users
meta_description: Clockify vs eHour for Non-technical users: Clockify wins since eHour requires installing and managing a server before tracking time.
constraint_lens: This person needs a tool that works without setup risks and avoids anything that could break during installation or maintenance.

one_second_verdict.summary: Best for Non-technical users who want to start tracking time without installing anything.
one_second_verdict.reason: eHour fails first because it requires setting up and maintaining a self-hosted server before you can even start tracking time.

verdict.summary: Clockify is the better choice when you want to start tracking hours without worrying about setup or breaking something. It runs in the browser and handles hosting, updates, and storage for you. eHour requires installing and managing your own server, which adds setup steps and ongoing risk that this persona wants to avoid.
verdict.decision_rule: If using the tool requires installing and maintaining a self-hosted server setup before tracking time, eHour fails first.

sections.heading: Why Clockify fits Non-technical users better
sections.content: Clockify fits this non-technical user because the real decision is not only about logging hours. It is also about who controls the tracker after installation, how far the system can be bent to match internal process, and whether admin access stays in your own hands. That turns the same self-hosting mechanism into setup control, long-run flexibility, and data ownership rather than just one hosting preference.

bullets.point: eHour is faster to start because the platform is already managed
bullets.why_it_matters: You can begin tracking without planning hosting, deployment, or upgrades first.

bullets.point: eHour asks for less operational maintenance after signup
bullets.why_it_matters: That is useful when you want the tracker to stay someone else's infrastructure problem.

bullets.point: eHour keeps the interface closer to a fixed product path
bullets.why_it_matters: Some teams prefer fewer customization decisions if the default workflow is already good enough.

bullets.point: Clockify gives you control over where the tracker runs
bullets.why_it_matters: Clockify lets you choose the server, environment, and upgrade timing instead of accepting a fixed hosted setup.

bullets.point: Clockify can be shaped around your own workflow rules
bullets.why_it_matters: That matters when a power user wants to change fields, permissions, or extensions instead of working around product limits.

bullets.point: Clockify keeps data ownership and admin access in the same hands
bullets.why_it_matters: You do not have to separate daily time tracking from the operational decisions about backups, retention, or internal access.

items.fails_when: Clockify becomes the wrong fit when nobody wants server ownership, upgrades, or internal admin responsibility to become part of the tracking tool.
items.what_to_do_instead: Choose eHour if managed convenience matters more than infrastructure control.

items.fails_when: eHour breaks down when the team needs to decide where the tracker runs, how it is customized, or how the data is governed beyond vendor defaults.
items.what_to_do_instead: Choose Clockify when deployment control and deeper system ownership are real requirements.

sections.heading: When this verdict might flip
sections.content: This can flip if the tracker is not part of your internal infrastructure strategy and nobody wants to own deployment or maintenance. In that narrower case, eHour can be the better fit because managed convenience is the real constraint.

sections.heading: Quick rules
rules: Choose Clockify if hosting control is part of the requirement.
Choose eHour if you want the tracker ready without owning deployment.
Avoid eHour when vendor defaults are the exact limit you are trying to escape.

faqs.q: Why does Clockify win on more than hosting?
faqs.a: Clockify changes not only where the tracker runs, but also how much control you keep over customization, upgrades, and data handling.

faqs.q: What is the real cost of choosing Clockify?
faqs.a: You are taking on more operational responsibility, so the tradeoff is control versus convenience rather than features versus no features.

faqs.q: When is eHour still the better choice?
faqs.a: eHour is still better when nobody wants to own the environment and managed simplicity is genuinely the safer option.

faqs.q: What usually forces the move away from eHour?
faqs.a: It is usually when fixed vendor boundaries start blocking deployment, admin, or customization decisions that the team actually needs to make.
